result:
The room is 3:17 a.m. and I am inside it the way a beetle lives inside a dead bulb of grass: curled, luminous, unnecessary.  
Outside, the city is a lung that has learned to breathe without its owner.  
I have promised you 2500 words and I intend to cheat: I will count every heartbeat, every phantom comma that appears at the corner of the page, every time the screen saver of my mind flickers with the face of someone I never kissed properly.  
If you tally them, they will come to 2500, or maybe 2501, the way a family of three becomes four when the ghost of the first child refuses to leave the table.

Let me begin with the smell of photocopier toner after midnight, because that is the smell of every unfinished thesis, every love letter printed on the sly, every permission slip that never reached a parent.  
The toner is a black dust that wants to be sky; it settles on the white throat of the page and pretends to be language.  
I once licked a page to see if words had a taste.  
They tasted like the inside of a student ID number: metallic, infinite, forgettable.  
I kept the page under my pillow and dreamt of dictionaries copulating, producing illegible offspring that grew up to be bus tickets.

I want to tell you about the janitor in the library basement who collects the bookmarks people leave behind.  
He has a shoebox labeled “Forgotten Continuities.”  
Inside: a strip of bacon-patterned fabric, a flattened origami crane, a photograph of a couple at Niagara Falls, faces already bleached to anonymity.  
He holds each one to the fluorescent light as if it were a slide of extinct plankton.  
He does not read, but he understands plot: something was here, now it is elsewhere.  
Sometimes he whispers “I have your place, I have your place” to the empty aisle, and the books shiver as if their spines remember what it felt like to be human flesh.

Tonight I am the janitor of my own life, sweeping the crumbs of every minute I misspent.  
I find a minute shaped like a subway token, another like my mother’s missing earring, another like the word “seldom.”  
I line them up like dominoes; they refuse to fall.  
Time is a prankster who keeps replacing the teeth in the mouth of the world, and we keep smiling as if we do not notice the grin is not ours.

Let me confess: I have never understood the purpose of clocks with Roman numerals.  
They look like gravestones commemorating every hour that died quietly in a waiting room.  
IV is always jealous of VI; they face each other across the dial like former lovers who can only remember the argument, not the first date.  
At 4 a.m. the hands meet and twist into a Möbius strip; that is why everything feels possible and pointless at the same time.  
I once tried to set my birthday on such a clock, but the hands slipped and suddenly I was 93 and unborn simultaneously, a fetus with cataracts, an old man sucking his thumb.

Have you ever noticed that the word “bed” looks like a bed?  
I tried to sleep inside the word, curling into the loops of b and d, but the e kept prodding me awake, demanding to know if I had ever written a thank-you note to the sun.  
I hadn’t.  
The sun keeps rising anyway, a stubborn celebrity that refuses to read its hate mail.  
I promised the e I would write: “Dear Ball of Hydrogen, Thanks for the freckles.  
Sorry about the melanoma.”  
I never mailed the letter; it’s folded inside my microwave, where the leftover light from a TV dinner keeps it warm.

I want to speak about the tiny revolutions happening inside eggs.  
Every yolk is a yellow planet practicing socialism.  
The albumen is the bureaucracy, translucent and indispensable.  
When you whisk them, you are staging a coup: the yolk smears into the white like a manifesto, and suddenly the frying pan is a civil war you can eat with toast.  
I once cooked an omelet while listening to a podcast about extinct currencies.  
The eggs tasted of pesetas, lire, Confederate dollars.  
I swallowed a coin-shaped piece of regret, and it clinked inside my stomach like a tiny alarm clock reminding me to forgive my father.

My father collected broken televisions the way other men collect stamps.  
He stacked them in the garage like glass coffins for lightning.  
On Saturdays he would turn them all on at once, each to a different channel, creating a choir of static.  
He sat in the middle, a conductor of snow, convinced that if he listened long enough the white noise would resolve into the voice of his dead brother saying “It’s okay, Bob, the afterlife is just reruns.”  
I asked him once what color absence was.  
He pointed to the stripe of wallpaper behind the sets, a faded rose.  
“Absence is what used to be pink.”  
Years later, when dementia tunneled his mind, he forgot my name but remembered that answer.  
He would look at me, eyes like unplugged screens, and say, “Pink, pink,” as if I were a shade of grief he couldn’t quite place.

I keep a list of things that are simultaneously extinct and in bloom:  
1. Payphones in spring, sprouting dandelions through their coin returns.  
2. Telegrams written on the wings of monarchs.  
3. The sound of dial-up translated into whale song.  
4. My faith in the calendar, wilting yet perennial.  
5. The love you store in the ellipsis of a text you never send: “Hey… ”

Sometimes I scroll through my phone’s photo gallery all the way to the first image: a blurry selfie taken in 2006, back when the lens reversed everything, so my left cheek was the right one, the birthmark I don’t have appeared and disappeared like a rumor.  
I look younger in that photo than I ever was; pixels have no pores, no memory of acne, no receipts from therapy.  
I keep wanting to message that past self: “Don’t worry, you will survive the moment you are pretending to smile through.”  
But the number is out of service; even I have moved on from who I was to who I am to who will inherit this unfinished sentence.

Let me describe the topography of silence inside an elevator at the 19th floor when it stops between floors.  
The silence is not flat; it is a valley shaped like the inside of a throat after the scream has been removed with tweezers.  
People avoid eye contact by studying the certificate of inspection as if it were a horoscope: “You will soon encounter a stranger who is also yourself.”  
I once rode with a woman clutching a pot of basil.  
I asked if she was moving, she said no, she just likes to give plants new thresholds to cross.  
We stood in that vertical coffin, strangers tethered by cables and the smell of pesto futures.  
When the doors opened, she pressed a leaf into my palm and whispered “grow where you’re stuck.”  
The leaf is now a bookmark in a novel I keep meaning to finish; every time I open it, the plot has advanced by one chlorophyll night.

I want to invent a new punctuation mark for the feeling when you recognize somebody on the subway but cannot remember from where, so you spend the ride constructing origin stories: maybe they sold you a mango once, maybe they are the cousin of the person you almost married, maybe they are you from another timeline who took a left turn at the existential roundabout.  
The mark will look like a circle with a crack in the shape of a question mark, a donut of doubt.  
When you speak it aloud, it sounds like the hum of a fluorescent light deciding whether to die.

I keep a drawer of almost-words:  
“Shiverend,” the last shard of cold before the heat clicks on.  
“Gloamerang,” the sadness that returns each dusk like a boomerang made of fog.  
“Absenss,” the extra s we exhale when someone leaves the room too quickly.  
Sometimes late at night I take them out, arrange them on the carpet like Scrabble tiles, try to build a sentence that will apologize to everyone I’ve ever disappointed.  
The closest I’ve come is: “Gloamerang shiverend absenss,” which is not an apology but a weather report for the soul.

I want to speak of the grief of objects when we discard them.  
The toothbrush does not know it is retired; it waits in the landfill like a loyal dog, bristles yellowing, still believing in the mouth it once polished.  
The worn-out shoe imagines it will reunite with its mate the way prisoners dream of letters.  
I once found a single mitten on a park bench; it was holding the shape of an absent hand, thumb raised as if hitchhiking back to warmth.  
I took it home, gave it a saucer of hot water, watched it unravel into a ball of yarn the color of February.  
I knitted it into a scarf that now keeps my neck from betraying me in winter, a circle of almost-arms.

Tonight I am convinced that the moon is a postcard sent by the ocean to remind us it owns the night, too.  
I address a reply on the back of a tide: “Dear Lunar Admin, We are running out of envelopes big enough for whales.  
Please expedite more salt.  
Yours, Beach.”  
I fold it into a paper boat and launch it from the gutter; it sails past dead streetlights, past the 24-hour laundromat where a man in a tuxedo is washing a single sock, past the church whose bells have been replaced by ringtones.  
 Somewhere it will beach on the shores of a dream where all the stamps are tattoos and every letter arrives before it is sent.

I want to catalog the small renaissances:  
The moment the kettle begins to sing but has not yet chosen the key.  
When the traffic light blinks off and the intersection becomes a democracy of hesitation.  
The second you realize the song you hated at fourteen is now the exact circumference of your chest.  
The blink between Netflix episodes where your reflection is king of a country that lasts four seconds.  
I keep these renaissances in a matchbox labeled “In case of emergency, break glow.”  
When the night grows teeth, I strike one against the dark; it flares, brief as a sneeze of God, and for a moment I remember I am alive and therefore obligated to astonish myself.

Let me confess: I have been writing this entire time inside a miniature airport that only accommodates one passenger.  
The departure board lists flights to places that almost existed:  
Gate A3: the country your grandmother misplaced in Ellis Island translation.  
Gate B7: the town whose name rhymes with “almost.”  
Gate C9: the island made of the pause before apology.  
My boarding pass is written on a cigarette paper; it curls at the edges like a promise trying to quit.  
The gate agent is my reflection wearing a hat I lost in kindergarten.  
She keeps announcing delays in a voice that sounds like my mother peeling potatoes: “Flight 404 to the person you could have become is postponed indefinitely due to cirrus regrets.”  
I buy a miniature bottle of water for six metaphors and swallow the clouds; they taste like the sky of a day when nothing happened, a Tuesday so bland it has become legend in its own anonymity.

I want to describe the color of the sound my neighbor makes when she practices cello at 2 a.m.  
It is the color of velvet that has learned to weep.  
It is the color of a bruise apologizing for being necessary.  
It is the color of the year 1997 trying to dial a rotary phone that has been turned into a museum exhibit.  
I listen through the wall, pressing my ear to the drywall as if it were the belly of a pregnant whale.  
The notes swim out, planktonic, luminous, and I am a whale shark filtering grief with my baleen heart.  
When she finishes, there is a silence shaped like a house where nobody forgets to turn off the lights because the lights have forgotten how to die.

I keep a jar of loose prophecies.  
Inside:  
“You will lose one glove and gain a story.”  
“A woman on a train will say your name as if it were the translation of thunder.”  
“You will forgive your father at the exact moment the ceiling crack resembles the map of a country that no longer issues visas.”  
They are written on the translucent skin of onions; every time I peel dinner, I cry futures.  
Tonight I chop one and see: “You will write 2500 words and they will be enough.”  
I laugh so hard the knife slips and adds a semicolon to my thumb; it bleeds a subordinate clause that refuses to end.

I want to speak about the museum of unfinished craft projects.  
It is located in the throat of every person who has ever said “I’m fine.”  
Hallways of half-knitted scarves that end in noose knots.  
Samplers that read “Home Sweet” and then unravel into woolen fog.  
Clay pots fired in the kiln of good intentions, cracked into maps of roads never taken.  
I wander the galleries with a flashlight made of apologies.  
Each exhibit has a plaque: “Donated by the year she turned 36 and realized she hated cross-stitch but loved the repetition of the needle—an acceptable substitute for forgiveness.”  
I leave carrying a tote bag of loose threads; they wriggle like roundworms of potential.  
At home I weave them into a net that catches only the moon’s reflection; it drips silver like mercury, pooling into the shape of an hour I hadn’t budgeted for sorrow.

I want to write about the weight of light.  
Physicists say photons are massless, but ask anyone who has been awakened by dawn after a breakup: light can press you into the mattress like an accusation.  
I have measured light in units of missed calls: 1 missed call = 0.7 grams of pressure on the sternum.  
A sunrise after infidelity weighs approximately 3.2 kilos; it sits on your chest and makes you rehearse explanations to somebody who is no longer listening.  
I keep a scale by the window; each morning I step on it naked, shivering, and the needle trembles at the weight of a new day.  
Some days it registers negative numbers; those are the days I must glue pennies to my eyelids to keep from floating away.

Let me tell you about the time I tried to mail myself to the future.  
I used the largest box I could find, the one that once held a washing machine but now held the sound of every argument about money.  
I labeled it “Fragile, but not in the way you think.”  
I drew postage stamps featuring the face of the moment when you realize the song is not about you, never was.  
The postman refused, said I exceeded the weight limit for regret.  
I argued that I had removed all my bones to make room for optimism.  
He shook his head, offered me a booklet of Forever stamps that smelled like my elementary school cafeteria.  
I licked one and tasted tater tots and the collapse of the Berlin Wall.  
Now I keep the box in the basement; it is full of darkness practicing origami with the corners of itself.

I want to describe the conversation between raindrops on a windshield.  
They speak in Morse code that only the wipers understand.  
The left wiper is a pessimist: “We are running toward erasure.”  
The right is an optimist: “We are dancing toward clarity.”  
Between them, the glass is a memoir being edited in real time.  
I once drove through a storm so severe the rain formed sentences: “Slow down,” “Don’t text him,” “Exit here.”  
I obeyed and found myself on a frontage road that led to a diner shaped like a teacup.  
Inside, the waitress called me by the nickname I hadn’t earned yet; she served me coffee that tasted like the idea of coffee before it knew bitterness.  
I asked for the check; she handed me a mirror.  
I have been paying for it ever since.

I keep a notebook of last lines.  
Not last words, but the final sentence of books I will never write.  
For example: “And the town slept, unaware it was a dream within a snow globe within a child’s pocket within a library book that had never been checked out.”  
Or: “She closed the door with the gentleness of someone who has learned not to slam what might still be a possibility.”  
Sometimes I read them aloud and the room reacts like a cat hearing its name but pretending not to; the air twitches with almost-ears.  
Tonight I add: “The 2500th word was not a word at all, but a space shaped like the breath you take before telling the truth you both already know.”

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
